sections.heading: What is WorkManager?
sections.content: WorkManager is the recommended Android Jetpack library for scheduling deferrable, guaranteed background work — tasks that must run to completion even if the user navigates away from the app, the app is killed, or the device is restarted. WorkManager abstracts over the underlying scheduling APIs (JobScheduler on API 23+, AlarmManager + BroadcastReceiver on older devices) and provides a unified, battery-efficient API for all Android versions. Unlike coroutines (which are tied to the app's process lifetime) or Services (which require manual lifecycle management), WorkManager guarantees execution by persisting work requests to a Room database and rescheduling them after process death or device restart.

sections.heading: When to Use WorkManager
items: Uploading logs, analytics, or user-generated content to a server when network is available
Syncing data from a remote server periodically or when connectivity is restored
Processing images or media files in the background (compression, watermarking, format conversion)
Applying database migrations or cleanup operations that must complete
Sending deferred notifications or reminders that must fire even after a reboot
Chaining multiple background tasks where step B depends on the output of step A
Any task where you need to add constraints: require charging, require Wi-Fi, require sufficient storage
Do NOT use WorkManager for tasks that need to run at a precise time — use AlarmManager; WorkManager may be delayed by the system

rows.workmanager: Yes — persisted to database; survives process death and reboot
rows.foreground_service: Foreground only — started service can be killed
rows.alarmmanager: Yes — but does not survive device reboot without extra setup

rows.workmanager: Yes — network, charging, battery, storage, device idle
rows.alarmmanager: No constraints — fires at exact time regardless

rows.workmanager: No — system may defer for battery optimization
High — batched by OS; respects Doze mode
rows.alarmmanager: Low — wakes device regardless of state

rows.workmanager: Deferrable, guaranteed tasks: sync, upload, cleanup
rows.coroutines: Short-lived tasks tied to app lifecycle

sections.heading: Core WorkManager Components
sections.content: WorkManager is built around four core concepts: the Worker (the unit of work to execute), the WorkRequest (describes when and how to run the Worker), the WorkManager instance (enqueues and manages requests), and WorkInfo (observable status and output of a request). Workers contain the actual task logic in a doWork() function that runs on a background thread. WorkRequests come in two varieties — OneTimeWorkRequest for single executions and PeriodicWorkRequest for repeated executions on a schedule.

rows.description: Abstract class containing the background task logic in doWork(); CoroutineWorker supports suspend functions
rows.example: class SyncWorker(ctx: Context, params: WorkerParameters) : CoroutineWorker(ctx, params) { override suspend fun doWork(): Result { ... } }

rows.description: Schedules a Worker to run once — immediately or after a delay
Schedules a Worker to run repeatedly on a minimum interval; minimum interval is 15 minutes
Singleton entry point — enqueue work requests and observe their status
Data class describing the current state, progress, and output of a work request
Conditions that must be met before the Worker runs: network, charging, battery, storage
Key-value input/output data passed to Workers and returned from them
rows.example: workDataOf("userId" to 42) — pass as inputData; return Result.success(outputData)

sections.heading: Creating a CoroutineWorker
items: Extend CoroutineWorker instead of Worker to use suspend functions — the preferred approach for Kotlin
Override suspend fun doWork(): Result — runs on Dispatchers.Default by default
Return Result.success() — work completed successfully
Return Result.failure() — work failed and should not be retried
Return Result.retry() — work failed but should be retried according to the backoff policy
Access input data: inputData.getString("key"), inputData.getInt("key", defaultValue)
Return output data: Result.success(workDataOf("resultKey" to value))
WorkManager automatically handles the threading — doWork() is called on a background thread
The Worker has access to applicationContext via applicationContext property — never hold Activity context
Maximum execution time is 10 minutes — for longer work, use a foreground service worker

rows.description: Work completed successfully with no output data
rows.behavior: WorkInfo.State transitions to SUCCEEDED; chained work continues

rows.description: Work completed successfully with output Data for downstream workers or observers
rows.behavior: Output data is accessible via WorkInfo.outputData; chained work receives it as input

rows.description: Work failed permanently — no retry
rows.behavior: WorkInfo.State transitions to FAILED; chained work is cancelled

rows.description: Work failed with error data for diagnostics
rows.behavior: Failure data accessible via WorkInfo.outputData for error reporting

rows.description: Work failed temporarily — should be retried
rows.behavior: WorkManager retries after the configured backoff delay; WorkInfo.State stays ENQUEUED

sections.heading: Constraints
sections.content: Constraints tell WorkManager the conditions that must be satisfied before a Worker is allowed to run. This is one of WorkManager's most powerful features — you can require a network connection, require the device to be charging, require sufficient storage, or require the device to be idle. WorkManager monitors these constraints automatically and runs the Worker as soon as all constraints are satisfied, even if that means waiting hours or days. Constraints help make background work battery-efficient by preventing it from running at the worst possible time.

sections.heading: Available Constraints
items: setRequiredNetworkType(NetworkType.CONNECTED) — requires any active network connection
setRequiredNetworkType(NetworkType.UNMETERED) — requires Wi-Fi or unmetered connection; use for large uploads/downloads
setRequiredNetworkType(NetworkType.NOT_ROAMING) — requires non-roaming network
setRequiredNetworkType(NetworkType.NOT_REQUIRED) — default; runs regardless of network state
setRequiresCharging(true) — only runs when the device is plugged in and charging
setRequiresBatteryNotLow(true) — only runs when battery is not critically low
setRequiresDeviceIdle(true) — only runs when device is idle (API 23+); use for non-urgent cleanup
setRequiresStorageNotLow(true) — only runs when storage space is sufficient
Combine multiple constraints: Constraints.Builder().setRequiredNetworkType(UNMETERED).setRequiresCharging(true).build()

rows.description: Passes a Data object as input to the Worker
Sets conditions that must be met before the work runs
Delays the start of the work by the specified duration
Configures retry backoff: LINEAR or EXPONENTIAL, with minimum delay
Associates a string tag for querying or cancelling work by tag
Marks work as expedited — runs as soon as possible with higher priority (API 31+)

sections.heading: PeriodicWorkRequest Configuration
items: Minimum interval is 15 minutes — WorkManager enforces this; shorter intervals are silently clamped to 15 min
PeriodicWorkRequestBuilder<MyWorker>(1, TimeUnit.HOURS).build() — runs approximately every hour
Set a flex period: PeriodicWorkRequestBuilder<MyWorker>(1, TimeUnit.HOURS, 15, TimeUnit.MINUTES) — runs in the last 15 minutes of each period
Flex period reduces battery impact by letting the system choose the optimal execution time within the window
Periodic work does not chain — use OneTimeWorkRequest chains for sequential operations
Periodic work survives device reboot — automatically rescheduled by WorkManager
enqueueUniquePeriodicWork() ensures only one instance of the periodic work runs at a time
ExistingPeriodicWorkPolicy.KEEP — keep the existing request; REPLACE — replace with the new one; UPDATE — update parameters of existing

rows.description: Enqueues a single OneTimeWorkRequest or list of requests
Enqueues a uniquely named OneTimeWorkRequest — prevents duplicate instances
Enqueues a uniquely named PeriodicWorkRequest
If work with the same name is already enqueued or running, keep it and ignore the new request
rows.example: Use for: avoid duplicate sync requests

rows.description: Cancel the existing work and enqueue the new request
rows.example: Use for: refreshing work with updated parameters

rows.description: Append the new work to run after the existing work completes
rows.example: Use for: sequential queue of tasks

sections.heading: Chaining Work
sections.content: WorkManager supports chaining multiple work requests into a sequence or parallel graph. beginWith() starts the chain, then() appends subsequent work, and enqueue() submits the entire chain. Output data from one Worker is automatically passed as input data to the next Worker in the chain. If any Worker in a chain returns Result.failure(), subsequent Workers are cancelled unless you configure otherwise. Parallel work can be combined using WorkManager.combine() which waits for all parallel work to complete before continuing the chain.

sections.heading: Work Chaining Patterns
items: Sequential chain: WorkManager.getInstance(ctx).beginWith(stepA).then(stepB).then(stepC).enqueue()
Parallel then sequential: WorkManager.getInstance(ctx).beginWith(listOf(parallelA, parallelB)).then(combinedStep).enqueue()
Output of stepA becomes inputData of stepB automatically via WorkManager's data merging
Use InputMerger to control how multiple parallel Workers' outputs are merged into one input for the next step
ArrayCreatingInputMerger — merges multiple values for the same key into an array
OverwritingInputMerger — default; last write wins for duplicate keys
If a parallel Worker fails, the chain fails unless you use a custom continuation policy
Tags can be added to chain steps: beginWith(stepA.addTag("phase1")).then(stepB.addTag("phase2"))

rows.description: Returns LiveData<WorkInfo> for a specific work request by UUID
Returns Flow<WorkInfo> for a specific work request — Kotlin coroutines friendly
Returns LiveData<List<WorkInfo>> for all work with a specific tag
Returns LiveData<List<WorkInfo>> for uniquely named work
ENQUEUED — waiting to run; RUNNING — currently executing; SUCCEEDED — completed successfully; FAILED — failed permanently; CANCELLED — cancelled; BLOCKED — waiting for prerequisite work

sections.heading: Reporting Progress from a Worker
items: Workers can report intermediate progress using setProgress(data) inside doWork()
setProgress(workDataOf("progress" to 50)) — reports 50% progress
Observe progress via WorkInfo.progress: info.progress.getInt("progress", 0)
Progress is available while WorkInfo.State == RUNNING
Use for showing a ProgressBar or progress notification during long-running work
Example: image processing reporting percent completion, file upload tracking bytes uploaded
setForeground(ForegroundInfo) inside a CoroutineWorker promotes the work to a foreground service with a notification
Call setForeground() at the start of long-running work to prevent the system from killing the Worker

rows.description: Cancels a specific work request by its UUID
Cancels all work requests with the specified tag
Cancels the uniquely named work request
Cancels ALL pending and running work — use with caution
rows.example: workManager.cancelAllWork() — use on logout to cancel all user-specific work

rows.description: In CoroutineWorker, cancellation is cooperative — check isStopped or use coroutine cancellation naturally
rows.example: if (isStopped) return Result.failure(); or use ensureActive() from coroutines

sections.heading: WorkManager with Hilt
items: Add dependency: implementation("androidx.hilt:hilt-work:1.2.x") and ksp("androidx.hilt:hilt-compiler:1.2.x")

Annotate Worker with @HiltWorker: @HiltWorker class SyncWorker @AssistedInject constructor(@Assisted ctx: Context, @Assisted params: WorkerParameters, private val repository: UserRepository) : CoroutineWorker(ctx, params)
Use @Assisted for the Context and WorkerParameters parameters — Hilt provides all other injected dependencies
Register HiltWorkerFactory in the Application class: override val workManagerConfiguration = Configuration.Builder().setWorkerFactory(workerFactory).build()
Remove the default initializer from AndroidManifest.xml: <provider android:name="androidx.startup.InitializationProvider" ... tools:node="remove" />
Implement Configuration.Provider in your Application class and inject HiltWorkerFactory via @Inject
All other Hilt-provided dependencies (repositories, DAOs, API services) inject naturally into the Worker
With Hilt injection, Workers can access the full dependency graph without using WorkerFactory manually

rows.mistake: Using WorkManager for precise timing requirements
rows.fix: WorkManager is not guaranteed to run at an exact time — use AlarmManager for exact-time alarms like calendar events

rows.mistake: Creating a new WorkManager instance with WorkManager() constructor
rows.fix: Always use WorkManager.getInstance(context) — WorkManager is a singleton; never instantiate directly

rows.mistake: Enqueueing duplicate periodic work without using enqueueUniquePeriodicWork
rows.fix: Use enqueueUniquePeriodicWork() with ExistingPeriodicWorkPolicy.KEEP to prevent multiple instances of the same periodic task

rows.mistake: Performing UI updates directly inside doWork()
rows.fix: doWork() runs on a background thread — observe WorkInfo from the UI layer and update there
Only retry transient failures (network timeout, server 503); return Result.failure() for permanent errors (invalid data, 400)

rows.mistake: Running work longer than 10 minutes without setForeground()
rows.fix: For work exceeding 10 minutes, call setForeground(ForegroundInfo) to promote to a foreground service worker

rows.mistake: Storing large data objects in WorkManager Data
rows.fix: Data has a 10KB limit — store large data in Room or files and pass only IDs or file paths through WorkManager Data

sections.heading: Summary
sections.content: WorkManager is the definitive solution for deferrable, guaranteed background work in Android. Use CoroutineWorker for suspend function support, Constraints to make work battery-efficient by waiting for the right conditions, OneTimeWorkRequest for single executions and PeriodicWorkRequest for recurring tasks, and work chaining for multi-step pipelines. Observe WorkInfo via Flow or LiveData to update your UI with progress and results. Use enqueueUniqueWork and enqueueUniquePeriodicWork to prevent duplicates, integrate with Hilt via @HiltWorker for full dependency injection, and always stay within the 10KB Data limit and 10-minute execution window.
